coredata.dc:title: Cell membrane-targeted near-infrared carbon dots for imaging of hydrogen sulfide released through the cell membrane
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Lan-Fang
Sun, Yu-Cheng
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: As a novel endogenous gasotransmitter, hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is involved in many physiological and pathological processes closely related to intracellular and intercellular signal transduction. Herein, carbon dots with near-infrared fluorescence (NIR-CDs) were prepared by the facile hydrothermal method. The near-infrared fluorescence peak of the NIR-CDs reached 683 nm, and the fluorescence intensity remained stable after storing at room temperature within 70 days. The prepared NIR-CDs can be directly used for quantitative determination of H2S with LOD of 56 nM. More remarkably, the NIR-CDs could specifically locate on cell membrane and have been successfully used for imaging of endogenous H2S efflux from cell on the cell membrane. This work provides a new strategy for the design of cell membrane-targeted CDs as well as the further study of the mechanism of gaseous signal molecules in signal pathways and cellular communication. Furthermore, we found that the distribution of NIR-CDs and the commercial cell membrane dye (DiO) on cell membrane has some difference, which may due to the complementary relationship between the NIR-CDs and DiO in solubility. This finding makes it possible to design fluorescent probes for targeted imaging in different functional regions of the cell membrane in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared carbon dots
Nanomaterial
Fluorescent probe
Hydrogen sulfide
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Imaging of H2S of living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 32070398 , 21777126 ). The authors thank the Large-Scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for the support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) particle size distribution of the NIR-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern. (b) FT-IR spectra of the NIR-CDs, the reaction products of NIR-CDs and H2S, as well as azure A.
(a) XPS survey spectra of NIR-CDs. (b−d) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and Cu 2p, respectively.

(a) UV-vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the NIR-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs (50 μg mL−1) under different excitation wavelengths from 550 to 670 nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the NIR-CDs (50 μg mL−1) upon addition of different concentrations of H2S (0-25 μM). (b) The linear relationship between F/F0 and the H2S concentration from 0.5 to 20 μM.
The change of F/F0 value before and after addition of 250 μM various relevant analytes. The concentration of H2S is 25 μM.

Confocal fluorescence images of H2S in MCF-7 cells. (a, c, e) Bright field images; (b) Red channel images (λex = 640 nm) of MCF-7 cells incubated with NIR-CDs (50 μg mL−1); (d) Cells were pre-incubated with NIR-CDs (50 μg mL−1) for 4 h then incubated with NaHS (30 μM) for 0.5 h; (f) Cells were pre-incubated with NIR-CDs (50 μg mL−1) for 4 h then incubated with Cys (100 μM) for 1 h. Scale bar: 20 μm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

MCF-7 cells incubated with NIR-CDs (50 μg mL−1) for 4 h, then further incubated with DiO (7 μM) for 25 min. (a) Confocal fluorescence images of NIR-CDs (Red, λex = 640 nm); (b) Confocal fluorescence images of DiO (Green, λex = 488 nm); (c) overlay of (a) and (b); (d) Intensity scatter plot of the NIR-CDs and DiO; (e, f) Fluorescence intensity of the regions of interest across the cells in the two channels (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).
Schematic diagram of the preparation of NIR-CDs and their application for the imaging of H2S released through the cell membrane.
Comparison of Different Materials for the Detection of H2S.

row.entry: lanthanide coordination polymers doped with CDs

head.ce:title: Cell membrane-targeted near-infrared carbon dots for imaging of hydrogen sulfide released through the cell membrane

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a novel endogenous gasotransmitter, hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is involved in many physiological and pathological processes closely related to intracellular and intercellular signal transduction. Herein, carbon dots with near-infrared fluorescence (NIR-CDs) were prepared by the facile hydrothermal method. The near-infrared fluorescence peak of the NIR-CDs reached 683 nm, and the fluorescence intensity remained stable after storing at room temperature within 70 days. The prepared NIR-CDs can be directly used for quantitative determination of H2S with LOD of 56 nM. More remarkably, the NIR-CDs could specifically locate on cell membrane and have been successfully used for imaging of endogenous H2S efflux from cell on the cell membrane. This work provides a new strategy for the design of cell membrane-targeted CDs as well as the further study of the mechanism of gaseous signal molecules in signal pathways and cellular communication. Furthermore, we found that the distribution of NIR-CDs and the commercial cell membrane dye (DiO) on cell membrane has some difference, which may due to the complementary relationship between the NIR-CDs and DiO in solubility. This finding makes it possible to design fluorescent probes for targeted imaging in different functional regions of the cell membrane in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: As a toxic gas molecule with rotten egg smell, hydrogen sulfide (H2S) has aroused much research attention since it was identified as a crucial endogenous gasotransmitter in biological systems [1,2]. In mammalian cells, H2S is produced from cysteine (Cys) catalyzed by cystathionine β-synthase (CBS), 3-mercaptopyruvate sulfur transferase (3-MST) and cystathionine γ-lyase (CSE) [3]. Endogenously generated H2S can participate in many physiological processes involving regulation of blood vessels, neuromodulation, anti-apoptosis, anti-oxidation, anti-inflammation and so on [4,5]. In addition, some reports have indicated that many human diseases including Down syndrome, Alzheimer's disease, diabetes, gastric mucosal injury and liver cirrhosis are related to the misregulation of H2S level [6,7]. H2S can readily across the cell membrane and participate in cell signal transduction, but the detailed function of H2S is not fully understood owing to its rapid catabolism and extinction [8]. Accordingly, the development of rapid, sensitive and simple methods to monitor the diffusion and intercellular transmission of H2S becomes extremely critical for biological systems.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as an emerging carbon nanomaterial have attracted great research interests in recent decade [9,10]. Owing to their simple synthetic routes, superior fluorescence properties, good bio-compatibility and favorable water solubility, CDs are a promising alternative to conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in the applications of fluorescent probes, bio-imaging and photodynamic therapy [11–15]. Furthermore, near-infrared emissive CDs (NIR-CDs, >650 nm) with long excitation wavelengths are superior, owing to their minimum auto-fluorescence interference, weak biological tissue photo-damage and deep tissue penetration [16,17]. Currently, significant efforts have been made in the development of CDs with NIR emission. For instance, NIR-CDs with emission wavelength at 704 nm were produced from lemon juice by solvothermal method, and applied to bio-imaging in vitro/vivo [18]. Through microwave-assisted exfoliation of red emissive CDs, the upconversion NIR-CDs with emission at 784 nm were prepared and further used for the vivo imaging [19]. A kind of NIR-CDs with emission at 685 nm was hydrothermally synthesized from o-phenylenediamine and dopamine. The obtained CDs were applied to the image of Fe3+ in living cells [20]. Unfortunately, only a few NIR-CDs have been successfully produced due to the unclear fluorescence mechanism [21,22]. To our knowledge, most of the prepared NIR-CDs are only used for bio-imaging and cannot be used to detect target analytes in cells. As such, it is significant to construct NIR-CDs based fluorescent probes and further applied to both quantitation and imaging of interested target molecules in biological samples.

To date, several fluorescent probes based on CDs for the detection of H2S have been reported. Du et al. prepared green emissive CDs with a fluorescence peak at 485 nm, the obtained CDs has been applied for monitoring H2S in living cells [23]. Bluish green emissive CDs at 485 nm also have been synthesized from sweet corn, and the CDs based strip has been developed for the detection of H2S in aqueous medium [24]. Through connecting blue emissive CDs with Ag nanoparticle, a “turn-on” fluorescent probe has been reported for monitoring H2S in brain microdialysate [25]. However, as far as we know, the previously reported CDs-based H2S probes emitted blue or green fluorescence at short wavelength region under the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) light. Owing to severe biological tissue photo-damage of UV excitation light and universal blue auto-fluorescence interference of biological matrix [26], this drawback is bound to limit their further applications in biological system [27]. Consequently, the employment of NIR-CDs should be better, because they not only have strong penetrability to tissues, but also can effectively avoid auto-fluorescence interference.

Considering the above facts, the NIR-CDs with emission at 683 nm were prepared through one-step hydrothermal method using azure A chloride and copper gluconate as the precursors. The constructed Cu-doped NIR-CDs can be used for rapidly, sensitively and selectively sensing H2S by using Cu as the responding site. But surprisingly, the NIR-CDs were found to have the location capability on cell membrane without any modification process. As a biological glycerophospholipid bilayer, cell membrane separates the intracellular and extracellular environments [28]. Fluorescent probes with the ability of cell membrane localization can be applied for monitoring the extracellular release of H2S. Consequently, the NIR-CDs were used as a cell membrane localization probe and have been successfully realized the imaging of H2S on the membrane (Scheme 1 ).
The reagents and apparatus are shown in Supplementary material.

A one-step hydrothermal synthesis method was employed to prepare the NIR-CDs. First, azure A chloride (0.0146 g) and copper gluconate (0.0227 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water (12 mL) via ultrasound. The solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave (25 mL) and heated to 180 °C for 6 h. Next, we centrifuged (6000 rpm, 10 min) the collected crude products to remove large solid particles, and then further filtered the obtained supernatant through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). After that, the resulting solution was dialyzed overnight with a dialysis bag (MWCO 500 Da) to remove the remaining chemicals, finally solid-state NIR-CDs were obtained after freeze-drying.

The fluorescence tests were conducted in phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 7.4, 10 mM) at room temperature. Typically, different concentrations of NaHS (0−25 μM) were respectively added into 50 μg⋅mL−1 NIR-CDs solution with a final volume of 4 mL. After 60 s, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 658 nm. The slit widths were 5 nm/3 nm. For the selectivity of NIR-CDs towards H2S, twenty-three kinds of potential interfering substances (250 μM) instead of NaHS (25 μM) were also measured in the same way. We repeated all measurements for three times.
The related content is shown in Supplementary material.

MCF-7 cells were cultured with DMEM which contained 1% penicillin-streptomycin and seeded in culture dishes at 37 °C for 24 h. For imaging of H2S, MCF-7 cells were pre-incubated with 50 μg mL−1 NIR-CDs for 4 h. After that, we rinsed the cells using PBS to remove the excess NIR-CDs, and then further co-incubated with NaHS (30 μM) for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the fluorescence imaging was performed immediately under a confocal microscope with a 40× objective. For imaging of endogenous H2S, MCF-7 cells loaded with 50 μg mL−1 NIR-CDs were washed twice using PBS. Then we incubated the cells with Cys (100 μM) for 1 h. After washing the cells with PBS for three times, imaging analysis was performed.

To verify the cell membrane localization ability of the NIR-CDs, MCF-7 cells loaded with 50 μg mL−1 NIR-CDs were further co-incubated with 7 μM DiO for 25 min. Then, using PBS to rinse the cells and imaging analysis was carried out.

According to the literature [29] and our previous work [30], the long wavelength emissive CDs would be produced adopting organic fluorescence molecules with long wavelength emission as precursors via hydrothermal technique. This may be due to the generation of large conjugated sp2-domains during dehydration and carbonization [31]. Thus, in order to obtain red emissive CDs, we conducted the following experiments.

First, azure A chloride (a biological stain, λex = 633 nm, λem = 676 nm) was chosen as one of the precursors to modulate emission wavelength. Furthermore, by the inspiration of the complex reaction between Cu2+ and S2−, we attempted to construct Cu-doped CDs for the sensing of H2S. Thus, copper gluconate which contains a large number of hydroxyl functional groups was adopted as another precursor, which may further improve the water-solubility and luminescent properties of the CDs. The CDs hydrothermally prepared using azure A chloride and copper gluconate show a bright NIR fluorescence at 683 nm under excitation of 658 nm. As expected, H2S can selectively quench the fluorescence of these Cu-doped NIR-CDs compared to other common cations, anions, and active species. Thus, the NIR-CDs can be used for detecting H2S by the “on-off” fluorescence signaling variation.

TEM image was adopted to characterize the diameter and morphology of NIR-CDs. Fig. 1 a indicates that the NIR-CDs are uniformly dispersed, and are quasi-spherical in shape. The particle diameter of the NIR-CDs is about 3.4–5.9 nm, with a mean size of 4.5 nm (Fig. 1b). The structure of NIR-CDs was further confirmed by XRD pattern. Fig. 2 a shows an intense broad (002) diffraction peak around 2θ = 21.5°, which is in accordance with the graphite [32,33]. FT-IR spectroscopy presents the surface functional groups of the NIR-CDs (Fig. 2b). The characteristic peak at 3425 cm−1suggests that OH and NH are present. The peaks at 1734 cm−1, 1636 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 1201 cm−1 correspond to CO, CN, CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. In addition, the stretching vibration at 1045 cm−1 is observed, which could be attributed to NCuN [34].

To further identify the structural components of NIR-CDs, we performed XPS measurements. The XPS full spectrum in Fig. 3 a reveals NIR-CDs contain C1 s (284.8 eV), O1 s (532.5 eV), N1 s (405.1 eV), Cu2p (961.1 eV) and S2p (171.9 eV), the corresponding atomic percentages are 65.07 %, 32.77 %, 1.14 %, 0.81 % and 0.21 %, respectively. The C1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) confirms that C = C/C–C (284.6 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), C–N (284.9 eV) and COOH (288.8 eV) are present. The N1 s spectrum in Fig. 3c can be fitted to pyridinic N (399.5 eV), pyrrolic N (402.0 eV) and amino N (401.4 eV). The Cu2p spectrum (Fig. 3d) exhibits the presence of Cu2p1/2 (952.2 eV) and Cu2p2/3 (932.4 eV), indicating copper ions exist on the surfaces of NIR-CDs prepared by hydrothermal method [35].

The optical properties of NIR-CDs were studied by measuring UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectra, fluorescence lifetime and QY. As displays in Fig. 4 a, the absorption peaks of NIR-CDs are located at 285 nm, 607 nm and 650 nm, respectively. The first peak at 285 nm is caused by π-π* transition of CC [36]. The last two peaks at 607 nm and 650 nm could be attributed to the n-π* transition of π systems containing CN/CN bonds [18,21]. Such characteristic absorption peaks cause the NIR-CDs to produce strong fluorescence emission [37,38]. Moreover, the NIR-CDs have the fluorescence excitation peak and emission peak at 658 nm and 683 nm, respectively. Fig. 4b shows the position of emission peak at 683 nm is nearly invariable when excited at 550−670 nm. It should be noted that the NIR-CDs have another emission peak at around 620 nm under the excitation of 550−600 nm. The results indicate that the NIR-CDs have excitation-independent emission characteristic, which mainly depended on their surface state [39,40]. Using cresyl violet as the reference, we calculated the QY of NIR-CDs is 5%. The stability of NIR-CDs at various conditions was also investigated, which can be seen in Supplementary material.

Under the optimal conditions, the response of the NIR-CDs to various potential interfering species including cations (e.g. Ca2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+), reactive species (e.g. GSH, Cys, Hcy, AA, NO, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, HSO3-, HSO4-, S2O3 2−, S2O5 2−), and anions (e.g. Cl-, Br-, I-, H2PO4-, HCO3-) were investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 6 , only adding NaHS (25 μM) into the NIR-CDs aqueous solution can cause a significant quenching effect on the CDs, while addition of other analytes (250 μM) has no significant changes in F/F0. The results indicate the high selectivity of NIR-CDs toward H2S over other relevant species.

Since S2− has a high affinity towards Cu2+, we speculated the possible detection mechanism is that H2S can react with the Cu2+ doped NIR-CDs, resulting in the surface structure of the CDs was destroyed and the fluorescence was quenched. To verify the above conjecture, we conducted the following experiments. As presented in Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity of the AG-CDs synthesized by azure A chloride and gluconic acid is nearly unchanged in the presence of NaHS. The results indicate that the fluorescence quenching of the NIR-CDs is closely related to the reaction between H2S and Cu2+ doped in the CDs. In addition, it can be observed in Fig. S4b that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NIR-CDs changes obviously after adding NaHS, implying the interaction between NaHS and Cu2+ doped NIR-CDs. We can also observe the formation of black precipitate after adding NaHS (Fig. S4c), which proves CuS is produced in the reaction.

To further explore the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime decays were also investigated in Fig. S4d. The average fluorescence lifetime of NIR-CDs is 0.72 ns by fitting the fluorescence decay curve. Upon addition of NaHS, the average fluorescence lifetime is almost invariant, suggesting a static quenching process occurs between the NIR-CDs and NaHS [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of H2S of living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before imaging, we first investigated the toxicity of NIR-CDs to MCF-7 cells. After incubating MCF-7 cells with NIR-CDs (0−300 μg mL−1) for 20 h, the cell viability is over 85 % (Fig. S5). Moreover, the cell viability was as high as 95 % when the NIR-CDs at the experimental concentration of 50 μg mL−1, which indicate the NIR-CDs have ultra-low cytotoxicity at the experimental concentration.

To investigate the biological applications of the NIR-CDs, we carried out living cells imaging experiments. As depicted in Fig. 7 b, the MCF-7 cells incubated only with NIR-CDs show a bright red fluorescence on the cell membrane, while the fluorescence intensity of the NIR channel decreases obviously when H2S was introduced into the cells (Fig. 7d). In addition, the NIR-CDs exhibit strong emission in the cell membrane region of the cells without penetrating the cell membrane into the cytoplasm, which indicates that the NIR-CDs have a specific targeting effect on cell membrane. The above results suggest the potential of the NIR-CDs capturing endogenous H2S efflux from the cell on membrane surface. Next, the practicability of the NIR-CDs monitoring intercellular transmission of H2S was tested. This group of cells was pre-incubated with NIR-CDs, and then further incubated with Cys which is a major precursor for the production of H2S by enzyme catalysis. It can be seen in Fig. 7f, the fluorescence signal in MCF-7 cells is significantly weakened after treating with Cys. This phenomenon can only be caused by the release of H2S from the cell through the cell membrane.

To further confirm that the NIR-CDs have the targeting capability in cell membrane, the co-localization experiment was carried out using NIR-CDs (red) and DiO (green, a commercial cell membrane dye). We can observe in Fig. 8 a-d that the fluorescence distribution region of the NIR-CDs and DiO is basically the same on the cell membrane. In the line-drawn region A (Fig. 8c), the corresponding signal intensity profiles of red channel and green channel (Fig. 8e) show synchronization. However, there are also some inconsistencies between NIR-CDs and DiO in the localization region of cell membrane. In the region of imaging inconsistency (line-drawn region B in Fig. 8c), the corresponding red and green channel signal profiles in Fig. 8f show location synchronization and intensity asynchrony in the line-drawn region, which indicated that NIR-CDs and DiO are both located on cell membrane but the distribution is diverse. The above inconsistencies in distribution may be caused by the difference in solubility between the NIR-CDs and DiO. The commercial cell membrane dye DiO is lipid-soluble, while the NIR-CDs are water-soluble. According to the literature [42], cell membrane is not uniform on the submicron scale, and it has different functional regions [43]. For example, lipid microdomains are high lipid regions of cell membrane that are rich in cholesterol, sphingolipids, and glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored proteins [44,45]. We speculate that DiO is incline to distribute in the regions with higher lipid solubility on the cell membrane, while the NIR-CDs are tend to locate in the regions of cell membrane that are less lipid soluble. Therefore, the complementary relationship between the NIR-CDs and DiO in solubility leads to some differences in their imaging on the cell membrane, which makes it possible to design fluorescent probes for targeted imaging in different functional regions of the cell membrane in the future.

As for the cell membrane localization ability of the NIR-CDs, we speculated that it may be related to their precursor azure A chloride. After finding that the NIR-CDs have the function of cell membrane localization, we used azure A chloride for cells imaging. The cells imaging result (Fig. S6) indicates that azure A chloride cannot enter the cytoplasmic region after incubating the MCF-7 cells for 1 h, but it could adhere to the cell membrane to a certain extent. It can be seen in Fig. S7, there are also some inconsistencies between azure A chloride and DiO in the localization region of cell membrane, which is similar with the co-localization imaging of NIR-CDs and DiO. In addition, the FT-IR spectra of NIR-CDs and azure A chloride (Fig. 2b) show that the synthesized NIR-CDs contain some basic structures of precursor azure A chloride, such as NH, CN, CN and CO, etc. In particular, it can also be observed in Fig. 2b that the FT-IR characteristic peaks of the reaction products of NIR-CDs with H2S are basically the same as that of azure A chloride, which further suggests that NIR-CDs contain some basic structures of azure A chloride. Thus, the cell membrane localization function of NIR-CDs is a continuation of the property of precursor azure A chloride, since azure A chloride can target the cell membrane, while NIR-CDs contain some basic structures of azure A. Currently, there are mainly three types of classical probes targeting cell membrane. The first type of membrane probes is developed by using lipophilic groups as the targetable units of phospholipid bilayer, such as cholesterol, tocopherol and alkyl chains. The second type of membrane probes is developed by using cell plasma membrane proteins as the targeting sites, which mainly relies on fusion protein tags, small molecule ligands, antibodies and aptamers [46]. Interestingly, however, the NIR-CDs do not fit either of these categories, suggesting that there may be other mechanisms as yet undiscovered.

In conclusion, we have synthesized near-infrared emissive NIR-CDs by a simple preparation process. The NIR-CDs can be used for the detection of H2S with fast-response of 60 s, low detection limit of 56 nM and high selectivity over other relevant species. Besides, NIR-CDs have the targeting capability on cell membrane and have been applied for imaging of endogenous H2S efflux from cell on cell membrane. The present work not only provides a new strategy for the preparation of cell membrane-targeted NIR emissive CDs, but also has the potential application value in studying the mechanism of H2S involvement in physiological and pathological processes.

Lan-Fang Pang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Yu-Cheng Sun: Software. Xiao-Feng Guo: Methodology, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Hong Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 32070398, 21777126). The authors thank the Large-Scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for the support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130403.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An endoplasmic reticulum-targeted ratiometric fluorescent probe for the sensing of hydrogen sulfide in living cells and zebrafish
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Shu, S. Zang, C. Wang, M. Gao, J. Jing, X. Zhang, An endoplasmic reticulum-targeted ratiometric fluorescent probe for the sensing of hydrogen sulfide in living cells and zebrafish, Anal. Chem. 92 (2020) 9982-9988.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Step-wise functionalization of polysiloxane towards a versatile dual-response fluorescent probe and elastomer for the detection of H2S in two-photon and NO in near-infrared modes
Chem. Commun. (Camb.)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zuo, X. Wang, Z. Gou, W. Lin, Step-wise functionalization of polysiloxane towards a versatile dual-response fluorescent probe and elastomer for the detection of H2S in two-photon and NO in near-infrared modes, Chem. Commun. 56 (2020) 1121-1124.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A mitochondrial-targeted red fluorescent probe for detecting endogenous H2S in cells with high selectivity and development of a visual paper-based sensing platform
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Song, Y. Wang, J. Ru, Y. Yang, Y. Feng, C. Cao, et al., A mitochondrial-targeted red fluorescent probe for detecting endogenous H2S in cells with high selectivity and development of a visual paper-based sensing platform, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 312 (2020) 127982.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA-capped silver nanoflakes as fluorescent nanosensor for highly sensitive imaging of endogenous H2S in cell division cycles
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xie, T. Fu, L. He, L. Qiu, H. Liu, W. Tan, DNA-capped silver nanoflakes as fluorescent nanosensor for highly sensitive imaging of endogenous H2S in cell division cycles, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 15404-15410.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A FRET-based upconversion nanoprobe assembled with an electrochromic chromophore for sensitive detection of hydrogen sulfide in vitro and in vivo
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Cui, H. Li, X. Ren, L. Xia, D. Deng, Y. Gu, et al., A FRET-based upconversion nanoprobe assembled with an electrochromic chromophore for sensitive detection of hydrogen sulfide in vitro and in vivo, Nanoscale 12 (2020) 17517-17529.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-ratiometric fluorescent probe for individual and continuous detection of H2S and HClO in living cells
Chem. Commun. (Camb.)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yue, J. Wang, J. Han, B. Wang, X. Song, A dual-ratiometric fluorescent probe for individual and continuous detection of H2S and HClO in living cells, Chem. Commun. 56 (2020) 2849-2852.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on fluorescent GFP chromophore analog for highly selective and efficient detection of H2S in aqueous and in living cells
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.L. Mittapelli, G.N. Nawale, S.P. Gholap, O.P. Varghese, K.R. Gore, A turn-on fluorescent GFP chromophore analog for highly selective and efficient detection of H2S in aqueous and in living cells, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 298 (2019) 126875.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A cell surface specific two-photon fluorescent probe for monitoring intercellular transmission of hydrogen sulfide
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Fu, H.W. Yao, X.Y. Zhu, X.F. Guo, H. Wang, A cell surface specific two-photon fluorescent probe for monitoring intercellular transmission of hydrogen sulfide, Anal. Chim. Acta, 994 (2017) 1-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on naphthalimide derivative-functionalized carbon dots for imaging lysosomal formaldehyde in HeLa cells
Nanoscale

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble carbon dots with blue, yellow and red emissions: mechanism investigation and array-based fast sensing application
Chem. Commun. (Camb.)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, J. Zhang, X. Zhao, W. Li, J. Wang, Y. Gao, et al., Water-soluble carbon dots with blue, yellow and red emissions: mechanism investigation and array-based fast sensing application, Chem. Commun. 56 (2020) 4074-4077.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lan-Fang Pang is currently studying for her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry at Wuhan University. Her research interests focuses on the synthesis of fluorescent probes based on carbon dots and their applications in biological/chemical sensing.
Yu-Cheng Sun is now a master student at Wuhan University. Her research focuses on the preparation and application of silicon quantum dots-based fluorescent probes.

Xiao-Feng Guo received his Ph.D. degree from Wuhan University in 2009. He is an associate professor in college of chemistry and molecular science of Wuhan University now. His research interests include computational chemistry and organic small molecule fluorescent probe.

Hong Wang obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Wuhan University in 1994. She is working in the college of chemistry and molecular science of Wuhan University since 1996. Professor Hong Wang is an expert in materials science and biochemical fields. Her research interests include fluorescent probes based on nanomaterials and organic small molecule.
